bids.reasoning: We are at the very beginning of the negotiation (t=0.0), so there is no reason to concede yet. The opponent's aggressive opener and threat to walk away is a standard cheap-talk tactic that I will ignore, especially since their offer provides very low utility for me across all issues. I will counter with my optimal bid to establish a strong anchor.

The opponent is relying heavily on aggressive threats and cheap talk very early in the negotiation (t=0.02). I will ignore these threats and assess their position strictly based on the numbers, which show almost an identical, low-utility proposal. Since it is still the very beginning of our negotiation, I will make a very small concession on my lowest-weighted issue (issueE, moving from valueB to valueA) to demonstrate some flexibility. I will hold firm on my highest-priority issues (issueA, issueB, and issueC) to maximize my expected utility until the opponent makes meaningful movement.

The negotiation is still in its very early stages (t=0.04). I will ignore the empty threats and make a small, controlled concession on `issueD` (one of my lowest-weight issues), while remaining firm on my highest-priority issues `issueA`, `issueB`, and `issueC`.

The opponent's aggressive messages are textbook cheap talk containing artificial ultimatums, which I will ignore since the time fraction is only 0.06. While their offers have shown a tiny concession on issueC (moving from valueE to valueD), their overall proposal still yields extremely low utility for me. Therefore, I will respond with a small concession on issueD to show continued flexibility, keeping my overall utility very high (around 0.94) as we are still in the early stages of the negotiation.

Because we are still early in the negotiation (t=0.18), I will maintain my highest-weighted preferences (`issueA: valueA` and `issueB: valueA`) while conceding on `issueD: valueE` to continue finding common ground on lower-priority issues.
